items.correct: Strong contrast between light and dark to model three-dimensional form.
items.why: Students think it's about subject matter (night scenes). Chiaroscuro is a TECHNIQUE using value contrast to suggest volume - exemplified by Caravaggio but used since the Renaissance to model form.

items.correct: An object's design should be determined by its purpose.
items.misconception: Decoration is the most important aspect of design.
items.random_wrong: Designers must follow strict government forms.
items.why: Students invert the meaning. Sullivan's modernist principle prioritises FUNCTION over ornament - the chair's shape should serve sitting, not the other way around. Postmodernism later challenged this.

items.q: What were Impressionist painters primarily trying to capture?
items.correct: The fleeting effects of light and atmosphere at a particular moment.
items.misconception: Their feelings or first impressions of a scene.
items.why: Students take 'impression' as personal feeling. Impressionism was about visual perception - how light actually appears on surfaces, painted quickly outdoors before light changed.

items.q: Which area is nearest the viewer?
items.why: The answer examiners want is: Foreground. “Background” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.
Correct answer: Guide the eye. “Block the view” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: How many ground layers (fore/middle/back) make depth?
items.why: Exact answer: 3. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
